title: Chapter XXVII.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: “Well,” said the Gaul, “I, for my part, though I am unequal to so great a task, feel constrained by those examples of obedience which have been related above by Postumianus, not to refuse that duty which you impose upon me.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: But when I reflect that I, a man of Gaul, am about to speak in the presence of natives of Aquitania, I fear lest my somewhat rude form of speech should offend your too delicate ears.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: However, you will listen to me as a foolish sort of man, who says nothing in an affected or stilted fashion.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: For if you have conceded to me that I was a disciple of Martin, grant me this also that I be allowed, under the shelter of his example, to despise the vain trappings of speech and ornaments of words.”

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: “Certainly,” replied Postumianus, “speak either in Celtic, or in Gaulish, if you prefer it, provided only you speak of Martin.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: But for my part, I believe, that, even though you were dumb, words would not be wanting to you, in which you might speak of Martin with eloquent lips, just as the tongue of Zacharias was loosed at the naming of John.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: But as you are, in fact, an orator, you craftily, like an orator, begin by begging us to excuse your unskillfulness, because you really excel in eloquence.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: But it is not fitting either that a monk should show such cunning, or that a Gaul should be so artful.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: But to work rather, and set forth what you have still got to say, for we have wasted too much time already in dealing with other matters; and the lengthening shadow of the declining sun warns us that no long portion of day remains till night be upon us.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: Then, after we had all kept silence for a little, the Gaul thus begins—“I think I must take care in the first place not to repeat those particulars about the virtues of Martin, which our friend Sulpitius there has related in his book.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: For this reason, I shall pass over his early achievements, when he was a soldier; nor will I touch on those things which he did as a layman and a monk.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: At the same time, I shall relate nothing which I simply heard from others, but only events of which I myself was an eye-witness.”
